commit.message:
8384135: C2: AArch64: Implement compressed jump table support with relative offsets

This patch adds jump table support to the AArch64 backend in C2.

In the current implementation, jump table entries stored full
target addresses, which increased constant table size and code
cache pressure. This change introduces a more compact encoding
on AArch64 by storing offsets relative to the beginning of the
instruction section:
compressed_offset = target_address - insts_begin

The jump table slot size is selected based on the upper bound of
the generated method code size. Since the final code size only
becomes available after shorten_branches(), the implementation
first initializes the constant table conservatively using 64-bit
jump table entries. After shorten_branches() completes, the code
size estimate is used to select the smallest slot size that can
represent all non-negative relative offsets:
* 8 bit if the code size is at most max_jubyte
* 16 bit if the code size exceeds max_jubyte but is at most
  max_jushort
* otherwise 32-bit

The constant table is then rebuilt using the refined slot size.

During code generation, the relative offset is loaded from the
jump table entry and added to the instruction section base to
reconstruct the final branch target address.

This approach also removes the need to relocate individual jump
table entries, since both the instruction section and the relative
offsets move together during CodeBlob relocation. Also, this
optimization is transparent to AoT cache relocation.

The optimization is enabled only on AArch64 through
the global constant use_compressed_jump_table.

Very small switch statements do not benefit from conversion to
a jump table. For such cases, use minJumpTableSize to reject
small switches and retain the existing implementation, avoiding
potential performance regressions.

The existing test compiler/c2/TestJumpTable.java verifies the
correctness of jump table generation and execution.

Tier 1 - 3 passed on both aarch64 and x64.

We can see some uplift from micro-benchmarks on Arm Neoverse N1 core:

Mode:  avgt
Cnt:   21
Units: ns/op
Uplift: Negative values indicate improvement

Benchmark                (distribution)        uplift
// Small switches: existing implementation retained
dense04SwitchCase           even                2.65%
dense04SwitchCase           defaultHeavy        0.00%
dense04SwitchCase           hotCase             0.00%
dense08SwitchCase           even                -2.85%
dense08SwitchCase           defaultHeavy        0.45%
dense08SwitchCase           hotCase             1.34%
dense09SwitchCase           even                0.18%
dense09SwitchCase           defaultHeavy        0.05%
dense09SwitchCase           hotCase             0.03%

// Larger switches: converted to compressed jump tables
dense1024SwitchCase         even                1.16%
dense1024SwitchCase         defaultHeavy        0.42%
dense1024SwitchCase         hotCase             0.27%
dense10SwitchCase           even                -6.14%
dense10SwitchCase           defaultHeavy        3.87%
dense10SwitchCase           hotCase             -1.20%
